uefi-rs `boot::exit_boot_services` is `unsafe fn exit_boot_services(custom_memory_type: Option<MemoryType>) -> MemoryMapOwned`; it internally retrieves the memory map, passes the correct map key, and retries (up to one retry) on a stale key; after it returns, all boot-services protocols and the pool allocator are invalid and BOOT_SERVICES_CODE/DATA become free memory; only runtime services + config tables remain. VERIFY against the UEFI spec ExitBootServices()/GetMemoryMap() section (uefi.org was Cloudflare-blocked this session).

The GOP (uefi::proto::console::gop) provides FrameBuffer as 'Direct access to a memory-mapped frame buffer,' with ModeInfo giving pixel format and stride; UEFI does no double-buffering. VERIFY that the captured framebuffer physical range remains valid after ExitBootServices and appears correctly typed in the final UEFI memory map (treat as reserved MMIO).

The rust-osdev `acpi` crate has a no-alloc API for bootloader use (find RSDP, enumerate tables, raw structs) and an alloc-gated higher level incl. PlatformInfo, PciConfigRegions, and an AML interpreter for DSDT/SSDT; the caller implements the Handler trait to map physical memory; entry points are AcpiTables::from_rsdp / from_rsdt. VERIFY exact module paths/struct names against the pinned version (sub-paths like acpi::madt::Madt 404'd this session; the top-level index is authoritative).

On arm64, ACPI is reachable ONLY via the UEFI system configuration table (ACPI_20_TABLE_GUID -> RSDP); the core enforces hardware-reduced mode (ACPI 5.1+), uses the XSDT and IGNORES the RSDT (32-bit-only, deprecated), uses only 64-bit FADT fields, and expects RSDP/XSDT/FADT/DSDT/MADT/GTDT plus MCFG (if PCI). Standards: Arm BSA (DEN 0094), BBR (DEN 0044), SBSA (DEN 0029).

ARM64 bare-metal entry contract: MMU OFF; dtb on 8-byte boundary, <=2MB, physical address in x0 (x1-x3=0); interrupts masked in PSTATE.DAIF; non-secure EL2 (RECOMMENDED for virtualization) or EL1; firmware must program CNTFRQ/CNTVOFF; SMP via spin-table (poll cpu-release-addr) or 'psci' enable-method where the kernel issues CPU_ON per ARM DEN 0022A; GICv2/v3/v5 setup required per mode.

Limine is a multiprotocol bootloader / reference impl of the Limine boot protocol, supporting IA-32/x86-64/aarch64/riscv64/loongarch64 and the Linux + Multiboot 1/2 protocols, handing the kernel a memory map, framebuffer, and RSDP. The rust-osdev `bootloader` crate is an EXPERIMENTAL x86_64-ONLY loader for both BIOS and UEFI (BiosBoot/UefiBoot/DiskImageBuilder). These bound the 'use an existing loader' option: Limine is multi-arch but external; the Rust bootloader crate is Rust but x86_64-only and experimental.

On x86_64, UEFI launches the boot image already in 64-bit long mode (paging + identity map on), so the bare-metal UEFI path needs no 32->64 trampoline - matching the long-mode entry tb-boot v0 targets. VERIFY against the UEFI spec 'Calling Conventions / boot environment' section for x86_64 (could not fetch uefi.org spec or OSDev UEFI page this session due to Cloudflare 403).

decisions.decision: Define full sovereignty as OWN + CONFINE, not reimplement: TABOS owns the machine, the hypervisor, scheduling, memory, agents, and policy; the unavoidable proprietary GPU/CUDA stack and the complex/unstable device drivers are confined to a least-privileged, disposable Linux 'driver/inference VM' behind the IOMMU. Adopt the Xen dom0/driver-domain + Qubes + pKVM-host pattern as TABOS's canonical L2 architecture. The sovereign boundary is ownership and confinement of the proprietary code, not its replacement.
decisions.rationale: Every serious sovereign/security OS does exactly this rather than rewrite drivers: Qubes is 'based on a secure bare-metal hypervisor (Xen)' with 'networking code sand-boxed in an unprivileged VM (using IOMMU/VT-d)', 'USB stacks and drivers sand-boxed in an unprivileged VM', and 'no networking code in the privileged domain (dom0)'. Xen driver domains give 'an unprivileged Xen domain ... responsibility for a particular piece of hardware [running] a minimal kernel with only that hardware driver and the backend driver'. pKVM 'isolates pVMs and Android into mutually distrusted execution environments' where the host (which holds the drivers) can be compromised without breaking guest confidentiality/integrity. This is the only architecture that squares 'full sovereignty' with a 20M-line driver/GPU reality.

decisions.decision: Do NOT attempt a native TABOS GPU/CUDA driver. NVIDIA's stack is unportable to a non-Linux kernel; instead pass the whole GPU through VFIO/IOMMU to a confined Linux inference VM that hosts the NVIDIA kernel modules + GSP firmware + CUDA/vLLM/llama.cpp, and treat that VM as untrusted-for-confidentiality.
decisions.rationale: NVIDIA's 'open' kernel modules v610.43.02 are still Linux .ko modules (nvidia.ko, nvidia-uvm.ko, nvidia-drm.ko, nvidia-modeset.ko), 'support ... Linux kernel 4.15 or newer', ship a closed OS-agnostic binary core (nv-kernel.o_binary), and 'must be used with GSP firmware and user-space NVIDIA GPU driver components from a corresponding ... driver release'; the kernel-interface layer is 'specific to the Linux kernel version and configuration' and 'must be built with the toolchain that was used to build the kernel'. There is no public porting interface to a non-Linux OS, and the CUDA userspace is wholly closed. Reimplementation is therefore infeasible; passthrough into a Linux VM is the documented path (cloud-hypervisor assigns NVIDIA GPUs via vfio-pci for compute). AMD ROCm has the same shape (Linux amdkfd kernel driver), so switching vendors does not remove the Linux tax.

decisions.decision: Make a hardware IOMMU (Intel VT-d with interrupt remapping / AMD-Vi / ARM SMMU) a HARD, non-negotiable requirement for TABOS L2/L3. Without it, any passed-through GPU/NIC DMA engine can read and write all TABOS and agent memory, collapsing the confinement boundary. Mirror Qubes: require both CPU virtualization (VT-x+EPT / AMD-V+RVI / ARM EL2) AND the IOMMU.
decisions.rationale: VFIO is explicitly 'an IOMMU/device agnostic framework for exposing direct device access to userspace, in a secure, IOMMU protected environment'; 'DMA is by far the most critical aspect ... allowing a device read-write access to system memory imposes the greatest risk'. The Xen driver-domain page is blunt: 'without IOMMU support, there's nothing to stop the driver domain from using the network card's DMA engine to read and write any system memory' and without it 'you cannot pass through a device to an HVM guest, only PV guests'. Qubes minimum spec mandates BOTH 'Intel VT-x with EPT or AMD-V with RVI' AND 'Intel VT-d or AMD-Vi (also known as AMD IOMMU)'. On ARM, seL4's VMM needs SMMU entries 'for any DMA-capable devices'. This is silicon-mandated: the IOMMU is the only thing that makes a confined proprietary driver safe.

decisions.decision: Make the IOMMU GROUP (not the individual device) the unit of TABOS device assignment, and require ACS-clean platforms on a TABOS 'certified hardware' list. The device-assignment policy and tb-hal PCIe code must enumerate IOMMU groups and refuse to split them.
decisions.rationale: VFIO states 'the group is the minimum granularity that must be used to ensure secure user access'; granularity is reduced by 'multi-function PCI device with backdoors between functions', non-ACS bridges 'allowing redirection without reaching the IOMMU', and PCIe-to-PCI bridges that mask devices. Concretely, cloud-hypervisor documents that an NVIDIA GPU and its HDMI-audio function commonly share one IOMMU group and 'it is important to bind both devices to VFIO and pass them both through the VM, otherwise this could cause some functional and security issues'. Therefore TABOS must reason about groups, and (like Qubes 'certified hardware') gate on platforms with clean ACS isolation so a single passed-through GPU does not drag a sovereignty-critical device into the untrusted VM.

decisions.decision: Build the L2 hypervisor as a minimal Rust type-1 microhypervisor that keeps the pKVM-style tiny privileged root (EL2 / VMX-root) but, unlike KVM/pKVM, retains TABOS-sovereign scheduling and memory ownership (Xen-like). The Linux driver/inference VM is a mutually-distrusted guest; the hypervisor's stage-2 page tables (EPT/NPT/stage-2) + IOMMU page tables must guarantee the driver-VM cannot CPU- or DMA-access TABOS or agent memory unless explicitly shared.
decisions.rationale: pKVM proves a privileged hypervisor can be three orders of magnitude smaller than the OS it confines: 'the EL2 portion of pKVM ... has three orders of magnitude less attack surface compared to the entire Linux kernel (roughly 10 thousand versus 20 million lines of code)' and it 'tracks the memory ownership of individual physical memory pages ... ensures that only entitled pVMs have the given page mapped in their stage 2 page tables'. But pKVM/KVM deliberately 'delegate workload scheduling to the host kernel' so 'a malicious host can choose to never schedule a guest' (no availability guarantee). TABOS wants the opposite ownership: it must own the scheduler and memory like Xen Type-1 (which schedules itself), while keeping pKVM's tiny-root + page-ownership discipline. Hedron/NOVA and seL4 show the same capability-based, user-mode-driver, mutually-distrusted decomposition is viable in a small TCB.

decisions.decision: Triage native drivers: TABOS writes native Rust (#![forbid(unsafe_code)] outside tb-hal) drivers ONLY for the small set of stable, well-specified, sovereignty-critical devices — virtio (already), NVMe (storage), one NIC family, xHCI/USB, and a simple framebuffer. It writes NO native driver for GPUs, Wi-Fi, or exotic NICs; those permanently live in the driver-VM. This bounds the bare-metal (L3) effort.
decisions.rationale: Drivers are 'the most failure-prone part of an operating system' (Xen wiki), which is exactly why every precedent isolates rather than trusts them. NVMe, xHCI, and virtio are open, versioned specifications a small team can implement and audit; GPUs and Wi-Fi are not (closed firmware, no spec, constant churn). seL4's CAmkES-VM only achieves passthrough for SATA/USB/Ethernet via per-device MMIO/IRQ/ioport config and notes the SMMU must be hand-populated — illustrating how much per-device plumbing native support costs. Confining the long tail keeps L3 finite and keeps the #![forbid(unsafe_code)] invariant defensible.

decisions.decision: Keep the TRUSTED display/console owned by TABOS itself on a simple, spec'd framebuffer (UEFI GOP / simple-framebuffer), and use the passed-through GPU HEADLESS for compute only — no display output path runs on the proprietary GPU stack. Agent UI and any sensitive rendering stay on the TABOS-owned framebuffer.
decisions.rationale: Qubes treats the GUI domain as security-sensitive ('Qubes GUI provides isolation between apps sharing the same desktop'; the GUI domain currently equals dom0) and recommends Intel integrated graphics, warning 'Nvidia GPUs may require significant troubleshooting' for the host display. Decoupling display from compute means the proprietary GPU never drives the trusted pixels. Cloud-hypervisor demonstrates that headless multi-GPU compute via passthrough is fully functional (GPUDirect P2P over PCIe on Turing/Ampere/Hopper/Lovelace), so inference needs no display path at all — the GPU can be a pure compute endpoint inside the driver-VM.

decisions.decision: Expose inference to TABOS-native agents only through a NARROW control/data channel (vsock or virtio over the hypervisor, qrexec-style), with the driver-VM serving a high-level inference API (e.g. OpenAI-compatible / gRPC over vsock) — never raw GPU/CUDA surface. This is the seam where the LLM-agnostic boundary lives: swap NVIDIA/AMD/CPU backends behind a stable API without TABOS ever touching a driver.
decisions.rationale: Qubes mediates all cross-domain interaction through the qrexec/Core-Stack RPC layer rather than shared device access, and pKVM warns that even with isolation 'the host's VMM ... is responsible for ... providing virtual devices' — so the interface must be minimal and policed. seL4's CAmkES-VM exposes exactly this pattern as 'cross VM connectors' (shared dataports + events between a guest and a native seL4 component). A narrow inference-API vsock channel keeps the proprietary stack from ever being a TABOS dependency surface and lets TABOS keep its LLM-agnostic contract: agents call an API, the driver-VM owns the GPU.

decisions.decision: Be brutally honest in the roadmap: the driver/inference VM IS a Linux, and the proprietary GPU tax is PERMANENT for local NVIDIA/AMD inference — there is no L3 'bare-metal, zero-Linux, full-speed local LLM' endpoint. Sequence sovereignty as: L1 = tb-vmm on a host /dev/kvm (TABOS still a guest of Linux); L2 = TABOS's own type-1 microhypervisor owning the machine with a disposable Linux driver/inference VM; L3 = native TABOS drivers for the simple devices only, GPU/CUDA forever inside the confined VM. 'Full sovereignty' = TABOS owns and confines the tax, not that the tax disappears.
decisions.rationale: The NVIDIA README forecloses a non-Linux GPU driver, so at every rung the GPU implies a Linux kernel somewhere in the box. The only escape valves are non-sovereignty-preserving in other ways: CPU-only inference (llama.cpp without CUDA — no proprietary driver, but far slower) or betting on a future open accelerator. The sovereignty win is structural: like Qubes/Xen, the proprietary code runs deprivileged behind the IOMMU with no access to TABOS memory, scheduling, or agent state — the host-compromise threat model pKVM formalizes ('properties hold in the event of a compromise within any pVM, including the host'). Stating this plainly prevents the roadmap from promising a bare-metal GPU outcome that silicon and NVIDIA's licensing make impossible.

gaps.gap: Prior TABOS docs (SOVEREIGNTY.md decided 'own VMM (tb-vmm on rust-vmm/KVM) + eventual bare metal') skip the middle rung entirely: there is no L2 'TABOS-owned type-1 microhypervisor + disposable Linux driver/inference VM' stage. The ladder jumps from L1 (guest-of-Linux VMM) straight to L3 (bare metal), but the GPU tax means the real terminal architecture is L2, never pure L3.
gaps.impact: The whole sovereignty endgame for GPU inference is undefined. Without the L2 rung, the roadmap implicitly (and falsely) promises bare-metal local LLM, and there is no design for the most important component: the confined driver/inference VM and the hypervisor that owns it.

gaps.gap: No IOMMU/SMMU + stage-2 translation (EPT/NPT/ARM stage-2) requirement appears in M0-M4 or tb-boot v0. The kernel has its own stage-1 page tables (MMU milestone) but the tb-hal has no second-stage paging, no VT-d/AMD-Vi/SMMU programming, and no interrupt remapping — all mandatory to be a safe type-1 host.
gaps.impact: This is an entire new privileged HAL surface (VMX/SVM or EL2 entry, VMCS/VMCB or stage-2 tables, IOMMU page tables, IRQ remapping). It is the long pole for L2 and is currently unscoped and unbudgeted; without it, passthrough is unsafe and confinement is fiction.

gaps.gap: No PCIe subsystem in the HAL: no PCI/PCIe enumeration, BAR/MMIO mapping, MSI/MSI-X routing, IOMMU-group discovery, ACS verification, or FLR/device-reset handling. VFIO and cloud-hypervisor both show device assignment is fundamentally a PCIe-group operation.
gaps.impact: TABOS cannot assign a GPU/NIC to a driver-VM, cannot certify ACS-clean hardware, and cannot recover a wedged device (GPU reset quirks) without this. It blocks every passthrough decision above.

gaps.gap: No device-driver scope/triage policy or native-driver budget. Prior research treats 'bare metal' as monolithic instead of splitting devices into native (NVMe/NIC/xHCI/simple-fb) vs forever-delegated (GPU/Wi-Fi/exotic).
gaps.impact: Without an explicit triage, L3 looks infinite and the #![forbid(unsafe_code)] / one-tb-hal-crate invariant is at risk; effort estimates for bare metal are meaningless.

gaps.gap: No agent<->driver-VM IPC / inference-API contract. The LLM-agnostic layer is asserted but the actual seam (vsock/virtio control plane, qrexec-equivalent, OpenAI-compatible API surface) is unspecified.
gaps.impact: This seam IS the LLM-agnostic boundary and the security perimeter around the proprietary stack. Undefined, it risks leaking raw GPU/CUDA surface into TABOS or coupling agents to a specific backend.

gaps.gap: No trusted-display story distinct from the GPU, and no measured-boot/attestation (DICE-style) for the disposable driver/inference VM image. pKVM/Microdroid show a confined VM still needs verified boot and per-instance secrets to be trustworthy.
gaps.impact: Without a TABOS-owned framebuffer, sensitive UI rides the proprietary GPU; without attestation, a tampered driver-VM image is undetectable, undermining the 'least-privileged, disposable' claim.

gaps.gap: No stance on the firmware/silicon layer BELOW the hypervisor: UEFI/coreboot, SMM, Intel ME / AMD PSP, and GPU GSP signed firmware. Qubes-certified hardware explicitly wants open boot firmware (coreboot) and treats these as part of the trust base.
gaps.impact: True 'own the machine' sovereignty is capped by closed firmware that is more privileged than TABOS's hypervisor (SMM/ME/PSP/GSP). The roadmap must acknowledge this ceiling or it overstates sovereignty.

gaps.gap: No evaluation of the consumer-GPU sharing limitation: consumer NVIDIA/AMD GPUs do not expose SR-IOV/MIG, so one GPU = one driver-VM (no multi-tenant partition without datacenter parts + licensing).
gaps.impact: Affects the multi-agent/multi-tenant model: TABOS cannot subdivide one consumer GPU across mutually-distrusted inference VMs, forcing time-sharing through a single (trusted-ish) inference VM, which weakens isolation between agent workloads.

hardFacts.fact: pKVM's privileged EL2 component is roughly 10,000 lines vs the Linux kernel's ~20,000,000 — 'three orders of magnitude less attack surface' — and it enforces memory ownership via guest stage-2 page tables it controls.

KVM/pKVM deliberately delegate workload scheduling AND physical interrupt handling to the host kernel (unlike conventional Type-1 hypervisors such as Xen), shrinking the TCB but giving up availability guarantees ('a malicious host can choose to never schedule a guest').

NVIDIA open GPU kernel modules v610.43.02 are Linux .ko modules (nvidia/nvidia-uvm/nvidia-drm/nvidia-modeset), support 'Linux kernel 4.15 or newer', ship a closed OS-agnostic core (nv-kernel.o_binary), require corresponding GSP firmware + closed user-space components, and have a kernel-interface layer 'specific to the Linux kernel version' that 'must be built with the toolchain that was used to build the kernel'.

VFIO requires an IOMMU for safe operation; without it a device's DMA engine can read/write any system memory. The IOMMU GROUP (not the device) is 'the unit of ownership used by VFIO'; ACS-less bridges, PCIe-to-PCI bridges, and multifunction backdoors reduce isolation granularity.

Xen driver domain: 'an unprivileged Xen domain ... given responsibility for a particular piece of hardware [running] a minimal kernel with only that hardware driver and the backend driver'; 'without IOMMU support, there's nothing to stop the driver domain from using the network card's DMA engine to read and write any system memory'; without IOMMU only PV (not HVM) passthrough is possible.

Qubes requires BOTH CPU virtualization (Intel VT-x+EPT or AMD-V+RVI) AND an IOMMU (Intel VT-d or AMD-Vi); confines the network stack in unprivileged sys-net and USB in unprivileged sys-usb via IOMMU/VT-d with 'no networking code in the privileged domain (dom0)'; recommends Intel integrated graphics and warns 'Nvidia GPUs may require significant troubleshooting'.

A discrete GPU and its companion HDMI-audio function commonly land in the SAME IOMMU group, so both must be bound to vfio-pci and passed through together; multi-GPU compute passthrough with GPUDirect P2P over PCIe is supported on NVIDIA Turing/Ampere/Hopper/Lovelace under cloud-hypervisor.

Hedron is a microhypervisor derived from NOVA (last common commit 2015) with capability-based authorization, user-mode device drivers/protocol stacks and a user-space VMM; it is x86-only (Intel VMX + EPT/nested paging), with AMD support currently removed.

seL4's CAmkES-VM performs device passthrough to Linux guests via explicit per-device untyped_mmios/ioports/irqs/pci_devices config; the SMMU must be hand-populated 'for any DMA-capable devices'; ARM guests require ARMv7 virt-extensions or ARMv8 with seL4 KernelArmHypervisorSupport and a virtual GICv2 (GICv3 under development).

decisions.decision: Architect tb-vmm as a deliberately UNTRUSTED, per-guest userspace VMM from day one, not a single trusted monolith — push all device emulation/VM-exit handling into it and keep nothing security-critical there.
decisions.rationale: Every minimal-TCB precedent splits the privileged mechanism from a per-VM usermode VMM. seL4 whitepaper: 'Each VM has its private copy of the VMM, isolated from the guest OS as well as from other VMs... the VMM cannot break isolation, and is therefore not more trusted than the guest OS itself.' NOVA does the same: the 9 KLOC microhypervisor only provides 'a simple message-passing interface that transfers guest state from the virtual machine to the VMM and back,' while 'the VMM emulates the complex x86 interface.' Designing tb-vmm as untrusted now means the L2 climb only inserts a tiny privileged core beneath an UNCHANGED VMM, instead of re-architecting a trusted blob.

decisions.decision: At L1, own only the device model + the tb-boot v0 contract; reuse the commodity VM mechanism (host /dev/kvm via rust-vmm) rather than writing a hypervisor kernel.
decisions.rationale: Oxide Computer — a funded, Rust-first, sovereignty-obsessed hardware company — did NOT write a hypervisor kernel. Propolis is literally 'VMM userspace for illumos bhyve,' reusing the in-kernel bhyve VMM through 'bhyve-api: API (ioctls & structs) for the illumos bhyve kernel VMM'; what they own is propolis-server + the omicron control plane. Google's crosvm (Rust, type-2, 'similar to QEMU-KVM') and Microsoft Hyperlight (Rust, embeds over KVM/MSHV/WHP) likewise own only the userspace VMM. The convergent criterion across all of them: own what defines your identity (device model, boot contract), reuse the silicon-adjacent commodity (KVM/bhyve). This validates SOVEREIGNTY.md's 'tb-vmm on rust-vmm using host /dev/kvm.'

decisions.decision: Anchor TABOS's sovereign identity ABOVE the hypervisor (tb-boot v0 contract + memory-centric agent model + forbid(unsafe) framekernel) so identity survives every rung swap L0->L1->L2->L3 and the substrate stays replaceable.
decisions.rationale: Genode is deliberately kernel-agnostic: the same component/security framework 'runs on most members of the L4 family (NOVA, seL4, Fiasco.OC...), Linux, and a custom kernel,' and Sculpt OS — built on that framework — 'is used as day-to-day OS by the Genode developers' regardless of base kernel. Qubes locates its identity in compartmentalization + driver domains, not Xen, and explicitly reserves the right to swap 'the Xen hypervisor... or - in the future - some other virtualising or containerizing software.' TABOS must likewise treat the VMM/hypervisor as a swappable floor, not its identity, or each rung becomes a rewrite.

decisions.decision: When climbing to L2, own ONLY a minimal EL2/VMX-root core and deliberately delegate vCPU scheduling and physical interrupt handling to the layer below — mirror pKVM, do not insource them.
decisions.rationale: pKVM's EL2 is ~10,000 LOC vs Linux's ~20,000,000 ('three orders of magnitude less attack surface') precisely because, per source.android.com, 'KVM... makes the explicit design decision to delegate workload scheduling to the host kernel. Given the size and complexity of today's schedulers, this design decision significantly reduces the size of the trusted computing base,' and 'pKVM also delegates physical interrupt handling to the host kernel to reduce complexity of the hypervisor.' The honest cost: this means pKVM 'doesn't provide availability to guests' (a malicious host can refuse to schedule). For TABOS, owning a tiny privileged core while delegating scheduling/IRQ is what keeps L2 tractable; insourcing them is the real expense hidden inside 'bare metal.'

decisions.decision: Set an explicit, falsifiable TCB budget for the L2 privileged core: target under ~10K LOC of privileged code (all of TABOS's unsafe+asm surface), with every emulation byte outside it. Treat exceeding the budget as proof the split is wrong.
decisions.rationale: Every minimal type-1 lands in the same band: NOVA's Figure 1 caption states 'NOVA consists of the microhypervisor (9 KLOC), a thin user-level environment (7 KLOC), and the VMM (20 KLOC)' — only the 9 KLOC runs in 'the most privileged processor mode (host mode, ring 0)'; seL4 is 'of the order of ten thousand lines of source code'; pKVM's EL2 is ~10K LOC. NOVA's was built by a small team and Cyberus productized its derivative (Hedron) as a 'Secure Virtual Platform.' TABOS's framekernel discipline (all unsafe in tb-hal, rest forbid(unsafe)) already creates the right boundary to measure against this budget.
sources: http://hypervisor.org/eurosys2010.pdf (Fig 1 caption; sec 4.1 '9000 lines of source code')

decisions.decision: Do NOT pursue formal verification of tb-vmm; treat forbid(unsafe) Rust memory-safety as the realistic assurance ceiling, and reserve any future verification effort exclusively for the L2 core.
decisions.rationale: seL4 is explicit: 'there is no need to verify the VMM, as that would not add real assurance as long as the guest OS, typically Linux, is not verified.' And the cost of real verification is brutal: seL4's functional-correctness proof was '200,000 lines of proof script' for a ~10K LOC kernel (~20:1), maintained for over a decade by a dedicated group, and even then the proof ASSUMES correctness of assembly, boot code, hardware, and DMA. forbid(unsafe) buys absence of memory-safety bugs but NOT functional correctness — be honest that TABOS gets seL4-flavored architecture without seL4-grade proof.

decisions.decision: Gate L3-baremetal behind a concrete security-identity test, not sovereignty maximalism: stay at L2-on-host (or even reuse a commodity type-1) unless owning the silicon directly is itself the product.
decisions.rationale: The whole field converges on 'own what defines your security/identity, reuse the commodity mechanism,' and the most sovereignty-driven projects still STOP short of owning the machine: Qubes runs on stock Xen yet is fully sovereign where it matters (network/USB stacks sandboxed in unprivileged IOMMU/VT-d VMs, 'no networking code in the privileged domain (dom0)'); Oxide owns the host OS and control plane but reuses the bhyve in-kernel VMM. L3 only pays for itself when silicon ownership is the differentiator (e.g. a confidential-compute root of trust / measured launch like NOVA's), which is not yet a stated TABOS requirement. Resolve: L3 is an opt-in capability gated on such a requirement, not the default endpoint.

decisions.decision: Offer a 'no guest kernel' micro-VM mode for agent workloads, where the framekernel IS the guest library — this is the natural shape for the memory-centric agent model and the cleanest way to delete the L0 bootstrap-OS dependency.
decisions.rationale: Hyperlight 'lets you safely run untrusted code inside hypervisor-isolated micro VMs that spin up in milliseconds... To minimize startup time and memory footprint, there's no guest kernel or OS. Guests are regular ELF binaries written in no_std Rust.' It explicitly is 'not designed for running full-blown Linux guest workloads.' For TABOS, where the workload is the agent runtime rather than a general OS, a kernel-free micro-VM removes the PVH/A0 32->64 trampoline dependency that L0 imposed and matches the MV milestone's goal of entering the guest directly in 64-bit long mode.

gaps.gap: The split-VMM pattern is missing. SOVEREIGNTY.md/MV frame tb-vmm as one thing; every minimal-TCB precedent (seL4, NOVA, pKVM) instead splits a tiny privileged mechanism from a per-guest UNTRUSTED usermode VMM. Prior TABOS docs do not declare tb-vmm untrusted-by-construction.
gaps.impact: If tb-vmm is built as a trusted monolith, the L2 climb becomes a re-architecture instead of 'slide a small privileged core underneath.' Declaring it untrusted now is nearly free and makes L1->L2 incremental.

gaps.gap: No TCB/LOC budget for the eventual L2 hypervisor. Prior docs say 'eventual bare metal' with no size target, while precedents are tightly clustered (NOVA 9K, seL4 ~10K C, pKVM EL2 ~10K).
gaps.impact: Without a falsifiable budget, 'eventual bare metal' is unscoped and unbounded; a budget (<10K privileged LOC) turns it into a gate the framekernel boundary can be measured against.

gaps.gap: Scheduling + physical-IRQ ownership is not separated from 'bare metal' in prior research. pKVM's small TCB depends entirely on DELEGATING scheduling and IRQ handling to the host; a pure L3 must insource a scheduler, IRQ controller logic, and drivers.
gaps.impact: Treating L3 as a single milestone hides that it pulls in scheduler+IRQ+driver ownership (and forfeits guest availability guarantees) that L2-on-host avoids. This is the dominant hidden cost of the last rung.

gaps.gap: The 'don't verify the VMM' criterion and the real cost of assurance are absent. seL4 says verifying a VMM over an unverified guest adds nothing, and its proof cost was ~200K lines for ~10K LOC over a decade.
gaps.impact: Prior docs lean on forbid(unsafe) as the assurance story but don't state its ceiling (memory-safety, not functional-correctness) or where verification effort should go if ever wanted (the L2 core, never tb-vmm).

gaps.gap: No explicit 'is L3 worth it' economic gate. The most sovereignty-driven funded teams (Oxide) and the strongest compartmentalization OS (Qubes) both deliberately REUSE the type-1/in-kernel VMM and stop at owning the layer above.
gaps.impact: The standing 'maximize sovereignty -> eventual bare metal' directive needs a counterweight: own-the-machine only when silicon ownership is the product (measured launch / confidential compute), else L1/L2 is the rational stopping point even for maximalists.

gaps.gap: Identity-above-the-substrate is not formalized. Genode's kernel-agnosticism and Qubes' Xen-replaceability show the winning move is to pin identity above the hypervisor; TABOS hasn't declared which artifacts (tb-boot v0 contract, agent/memory model, forbid(unsafe) discipline) ARE the invariant identity vs the swappable floor.
gaps.impact: Without that declaration, each rung risks being treated as a new identity rather than a substrate swap under a stable identity, multiplying rewrite cost across L0->L3.

hardFacts.fact: NOVA microhypervisor is 'approximately 9000 lines of source code' running in the most privileged mode (host mode, ring 0); full TCB split is 9 KLOC microhypervisor + 7 KLOC thin user-level environment + 20 KLOC per-VM VMM; each VM has its own usermode VMM that emulates the x86 interface over a simple message-passing interface.
hardFacts.source: http://hypervisor.org/eurosys2010.pdf (NOVA, EuroSys 2010, sec 4.1 and Figure 1 caption)

hardFacts.fact: Hedron is Cyberus Technology's NOVA derivative (last common commit with NOVA dates from 2015), combines microkernel+hypervisor with a capability-based authorization model, runs unmodified guests via a userspace VMM, is the core of Cyberus's 'Secure Virtual Platform,' x86-only (AMD support removed), requires ACPI/XSAVE/FSGSBASE + VMX/EPT.

pKVM's EL2 portion is roughly 10 thousand lines of code vs the entire Linux kernel's ~20 million (three orders of magnitude less attack surface); pKVM delegates BOTH vCPU scheduling and physical interrupt handling to the host kernel to shrink the TCB, and consequently 'doesn't provide availability to guests' (a malicious host can refuse to schedule a guest).

In Android's AVF, the VMM is crosvm (a Rust type-2 VMM, each virtual device sandboxed in a minijail with a seccomp syscall policy) and the hypervisor is pKVM; the host Android, guest pVMs, and hypervisor are mutually distrusted, with per-pVM pvmfw + DICE-derived secrets.

seL4's functional-correctness proof was ~200,000 lines of proof script for a kernel 'of the order of ten thousand lines of source code'; it runs as a hypervisor in EL2 (Arm) / Root Ring-0 (x86) / HS (RISC-V); its per-VM usermode VMM is deliberately NOT verified ('no need to verify the VMM... as long as the guest OS, typically Linux, is not verified').
seL4's proofs explicitly ASSUME the correctness of assembly code, boot code, the machine interface, hardware, and DMA — these are outside the verified scope.

Oxide's Propolis is a Rust userspace VMM ('VMM userspace for illumos bhyve') that reuses the illumos in-kernel bhyve VMM via the bhyve-api ioctl crate; Oxide owns propolis-server (REST-operated) + the omicron control plane, runs best on AMD hosts, MPL-2.0 licensed.

Genode is base-kernel-agnostic, running on NOVA, seL4, Fiasco.OC, OKL4, L4ka::Pistachio, L4/Fiasco, Linux, and its own custom kernel, with a custom ARM VMM and VirtualBox-on-NOVA; Sculpt OS (built on Genode) 'is used as day-to-day OS by the Genode developers'; NOVA is the default kernel for Sculpt.

Qubes reuses Xen as a bare-metal type-1 hypervisor and owns the compartmentalization layer: networking and USB stacks are sandboxed in unprivileged VMs via IOMMU/VT-d, dom0 has no networking code, plus a GUI-virtualization protocol; Qubes explicitly reserves the option to replace Xen 'in the future' with other virtualizing/containerizing technology.

Hyperlight is an embeddable Rust micro-VMM with NO guest kernel/OS (guest = no_std Rust/C ELF + guest library, ms startup, microsecond calls) over KVM/MSHV/Windows Hypervisor Platform; explicitly NOT for full Linux guests. Bareflank is a Rust/C++ hypervisor SDK (by AIS) providing only enough to start/stop a hypervisor — a 'ring -1'/VMX-root microkernel plus your extension; the full product is MicroV.
